description: Set up GitHub Actions CI/CD workflows for the OpenTabs monorepo. Add a main CI workflow that runs on every push and PR with build, type-check, lint, format-check, knip, unit tests, and E2E tests. Add a separate docs CI workflow. Add a plugin quality check workflow. All workflows run on ubuntu-latest (free for public repos). E2E tests use xvfb-run since Playwright needs a display for headed Chromium with extensions.

userStories.title: Add main CI workflow for platform quality checks
userStories.description: Create a GitHub Actions workflow that runs the full platform quality check suite (build, type-check, lint, format-check, knip, unit tests) on every push to main and on every pull request.
acceptanceCriteria: Workflow file exists at .github/workflows/ci.yml
Triggers on push to main and on pull_request
Runs on ubuntu-latest with Node.js 22
Caches node_modules via actions/cache for faster installs
Runs: npm ci, npm run build, npm run lint, npm run format:check, npm run knip, npm run test
Uses concurrency group to cancel redundant runs on the same PR
Storybook build is included (npm run storybook:build)
userStories.notes:
Files:
- .github/workflows/ci.yml (new file)

Context:
Two workflows already exist:
- `.github/workflows/deploy-docs.yml` — deploys docs to Cloudflare on push to main (paths: docs/**)
- `.github/workflows/publish-plugins.yml` — publishes changed plugins to npm on push to main (paths: plugins/*/src/**, plugins/*/package.json, plugins/*/icon.svg)

Neither runs quality checks. The `npm run check` script does: build + build:plugins + storybook:build + lint + format:check + knip + test.

For this story, create a CI workflow that runs the platform checks (NOT E2E — that's US-002, and NOT plugins — that's US-003).

Workflow structure:
```yaml
name: CI

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
  pull_request:

concurrency:
  group: ci-${{ github.ref }}
  cancel-in-progress: true

jobs:
  check:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: 22

      - name: Cache node_modules
        uses: actions/cache@v4
        with:
          path: node_modules
          key: node-modules-${{ hashFiles('package-lock.json') }}
          restore-keys: node-modules-

      - name: Install dependencies
        run: npm ci

      - name: Build
        run: npm run build

      - name: Storybook build
        run: npm run storybook:build

      - name: Lint
        run: npm run lint

      - name: Format check
        run: npm run format:check

      - name: Unused code check
        run: npm run knip

      - name: Unit tests
        run: npm run test
```

Notes on npm ci vs npm install: use `npm ci` in CI for deterministic installs from package-lock.json. It's faster and more reliable.

The `npm run build` command includes tsc --build, browser extension bundling (esbuild), icon generation (sharp for PNG), and extension installation. All of these should work on ubuntu-latest with Node.js 22.

The `npm run storybook:build` compiles the Storybook static site — it verifies all story files compile and the side panel components render without errors.

Constraints:
- Do NOT include E2E tests in this job (they need special setup — see US-002)
- Do NOT include plugin checks (they have separate node_modules — see US-003)
- Do NOT include docs checks (separate workflow — see US-004)
- The `sharp` npm package (used for icon generation) may need system dependencies on Linux. If the build fails on the icon step, add `--ignore-scripts` or install libvips. However, sharp v0.33+ includes prebuilt binaries for linux-x64 so it should work out of the box.
- Use `npm ci` not `npm install`

userStories.title: Add E2E test job to the CI workflow
userStories.description: Add a separate job in the CI workflow that runs E2E tests using Playwright with Chromium. E2E tests require a headed browser with extension support, so use xvfb-run to provide a virtual display.
acceptanceCriteria: E2E job runs in the ci.yml workflow as a separate job that depends on the check job
Uses the official Playwright Docker image or installs Playwright browsers
Runs E2E tests via xvfb-run for headed Chromium with extension support
Uploads Playwright test results (HTML report, traces) as artifacts on failure
E2E job has a longer timeout (30 minutes) to accommodate slow runs
The e2e-test plugin is built before tests run
userStories.notes:
Files:
- .github/workflows/ci.yml (add e2e job)

Context:
The E2E tests use `chromium.launchPersistentContext` with `headless: false` because Chrome extensions require headed mode. On GitHub Actions (ubuntu-latest), there's no display server by default. Use `xvfb-run` to provide a virtual framebuffer.

The `npm run test:e2e` script does:
```bash
cd plugins/e2e-test && npm ci && rm -f tsconfig.tsbuildinfo && OPENTABS_CONFIG_DIR=/tmp/opentabs-e2e-prebuild npm run build && cd ../.. && npx playwright test
```

It builds the e2e-test plugin first, then runs Playwright.

Playwright config (playwright.config.ts):
- `workers: process.env.CI ? 2 : 4` — already reduces parallelism in CI
- `retries: 2` — retries flaky tests
- `forbidOnly: !!process.env.CI` — fails if `.only` is left in
- Reports: list + html (open: 'never')

E2E job structure:
```yaml
  e2e:
    needs: check
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    timeout-minutes: 30
    env:
      CI: '1'
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: 22

      - name: Cache node_modules
        uses: actions/cache@v4
        with:
          path: node_modules
          key: node-modules-${{ hashFiles('package-lock.json') }}
          restore-keys: node-modules-

      - name: Install dependencies
        run: npm ci

      - name: Build
        run: npm run build

      - name: Install Playwright browsers
        run: npx playwright install chromium --with-deps

      - name: Run E2E tests
        run: xvfb-run --auto-servernum npm run test:e2e

      - name: Upload Playwright report
        if: failure()
        uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
        with:
          name: playwright-report
          path: |
            playwright-report/
            test-results/
          retention-days: 7
```

Key details:
- `needs: check` — E2E only runs if the fast checks pass. No point running expensive E2E if lint/build fails.
- `xvfb-run --auto-servernum` — allocates a virtual display. Required for headed Chromium.
- `npx playwright install chromium --with-deps` — installs Chromium and system dependencies (fonts, libs). The `--with-deps` flag installs OS packages needed by Chromium.
- `CI: '1'` — sets CI env var which Playwright uses to reduce workers to 2.
- Upload artifacts only on failure — Playwright's HTML report and test traces help debug CI failures.

Constraints:
- Do NOT use a Docker container job — running in the GitHub Actions VM directly with xvfb-run is simpler and has better caching
- The e2e-test plugin build is handled by `npm run test:e2e` itself — no extra step needed
- Playwright HTML report goes to `playwright-report/`, traces go to `test-results/`

userStories.title: Add plugin quality check job to CI workflow
userStories.description: Add a job that builds and checks all example plugins (plugins/) to catch breakage from platform changes.
acceptanceCriteria: Plugin check job runs in ci.yml as a separate job that depends on the check job
Runs npm run build:plugins to build all plugins
Runs npm run check:plugins to type-check and lint all plugins
Caches plugin node_modules separately
userStories.notes:
Files:
- .github/workflows/ci.yml (add plugins job)

Context:
Plugins are standalone projects (NOT in npm workspaces). Each has its own node_modules and package-lock.json. The root scripts handle iterating over them:
- `npm run build:plugins` — runs `tsx scripts/plugins.ts --build` which installs + builds each plugin
- `npm run check:plugins` — runs `tsx scripts/plugins.ts --check` which type-checks + lints each plugin

The plugins depend on published `@opentabs-dev/*` packages from npm, not local workspace links. So they need the platform packages to be published OR the build step needs to work with the npm versions.

Actually, looking at the build:plugins script, it does `npm install` in each plugin dir which pulls from npm. This should work in CI as long as the npm packages are published.

Job structure:
```yaml
  plugins:
    needs: check
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: 22

      - name: Cache node_modules
        uses: actions/cache@v4
        with:
          path: node_modules
          key: node-modules-${{ hashFiles('package-lock.json') }}
          restore-keys: node-modules-

      - name: Install dependencies
        run: npm ci

      - name: Build platform
        run: npm run build

      - name: Build plugins
        run: npm run build:plugins

      - name: Check plugins
        run: npm run check:plugins
```

Note: `build:plugins` runs `npm install` inside each plugin, which downloads from npm. The platform must be built first because `opentabs-plugin build` (from plugin-tools) is used during plugin builds.

Constraints:
- Plugin node_modules caching is harder (multiple dirs). For v1, skip plugin-specific caching — the build:plugins script handles fresh installs.
- If a plugin depends on a platform version not yet published, the build will fail. This is expected — plugins should always use published versions.

userStories.title: Add docs quality check workflow
userStories.description: Add a CI job or workflow that checks the docs site (build, type-check, lint, knip, format-check) on PRs that modify docs/.
acceptanceCriteria: Docs check runs on pull_request when docs/ files change
Also runs on push to main when docs/ files change
Runs: npm run check:docs (which delegates to docs/package.json check script)
Does not run when only platform/ or plugins/ change
userStories.notes:
Files:
- .github/workflows/ci.yml (add docs job) OR .github/workflows/ci-docs.yml (new file)

Context:
The existing `deploy-docs.yml` only builds and deploys — it doesn't run quality checks. The `npm run check:docs` script delegates to `docs/package.json` which runs build + type-check + lint + knip + format:check.

Two design options:
1. Add a `docs` job to ci.yml with `paths` filter — but ci.yml triggers on ALL pushes/PRs, adding a skipped docs job to every run
2. Create a separate ci-docs.yml with paths filter — cleaner, only triggers when docs change

Option 2 is cleaner. Create `.github/workflows/ci-docs.yml`:
```yaml
name: CI — Docs

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
    paths: ['docs/**']
  pull_request:
    paths: ['docs/**']

concurrency:
  group: ci-docs-${{ github.ref }}
  cancel-in-progress: true

jobs:
  check:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: 22

      - name: Install dependencies
        run: npm install
        working-directory: docs

      - name: Check docs (build + type-check + lint + knip + format)
        run: npm run check
        working-directory: docs
```

Docs is a standalone Next.js project with its own node_modules. Use `working-directory: docs` and `npm install` (not npm ci — check if docs has a lockfile).

Actually, check if docs has a package-lock.json:
- If yes, use `npm ci` for deterministic builds
- If no, use `npm install`

Constraints:
- Keep this separate from ci.yml to avoid noise on non-docs PRs
- The existing deploy-docs.yml handles deployment — this workflow only checks quality
- Do NOT modify deploy-docs.yml

userStories.title: Add CI status badges to README
userStories.description: Add GitHub Actions CI status badges to the README.md so build status is visible at a glance.
acceptanceCriteria: README.md shows a CI badge that links to the workflow runs
Badge shows the current status of the main branch CI
Badge is placed near the top of the README alongside any existing badges
userStories.notes:
Files:
- README.md

Context:
The README already has badges. Search for existing badge markdown (likely near the top). Add the CI badge alongside them.

Badge markdown:
```md
[![CI](https://github.com/opentabs-dev/opentabs/actions/workflows/ci.yml/badge.svg)](https://github.com/opentabs-dev/opentabs/actions/workflows/ci.yml)
```

If there's a docs CI workflow too:
```md
[![Docs](https://github.com/opentabs-dev/opentabs/actions/workflows/ci-docs.yml/badge.svg)](https://github.com/opentabs-dev/opentabs/actions/workflows/ci-docs.yml)
```

Constraints:
- Place badges on the same line as existing badges
- Do NOT modify any other README content

userStories.title: Add unit tests verifying CI workflow YAML validity
userStories.description: Add a unit test that validates the CI workflow YAML files are syntactically correct and have the expected structure.
acceptanceCriteria: Unit test reads all .github/workflows/*.yml files
Validates each file is valid YAML
Validates each workflow has required fields: name, on, jobs
Validates each job has runs-on and steps
Test passes with the new CI workflow files
userStories.notes:
Files:
- platform/mcp-server/src/ci-workflows.test.ts (new file) OR a more appropriate location

Context:
Workflow YAML validity is important because a malformed workflow file silently fails to trigger. A unit test catches this early.

However, the monorepo uses Vitest for tests and the workflow files are YAML. We need a YAML parser. Options:
1. Use `yaml` npm package (check if already a dependency)
2. Use a simple regex/string check (less robust)
3. Use `js-yaml` package

Check if any YAML parser is already in dependencies. If not, a simpler approach: just verify the files exist and are non-empty, and use `JSON.parse` on `JSON.stringify` round-trip if we can find a YAML parser.

Actually, the simplest approach that adds value without new dependencies: read each .yml file, verify it exists, verify it has expected string patterns (name:, on:, jobs:, runs-on:, steps:). This is a smoke test, not full validation.

Alternatively, consider adding this as a lint rule or a script rather than a unit test. But a unit test is the most natural fit in this codebase.

The test file location: since this tests infrastructure (not a specific package), put it in a shared location. Could be `platform/shared/src/ci-workflows.test.ts` or a new top-level test. Check the tsconfig coverage rules to ensure the test file is included.

Constraints:
- Do NOT add new npm dependencies just for YAML parsing
- A smoke test (string pattern matching) is sufficient
- Ensure the test file is covered by a tsconfig that tsc --build reaches
